volumeTitle: The Little Carthaginian. Pseudolus. The Rope
label: The Little Carthaginian (Section 1.1.169c - Section 1.2.281b)

sections.label: Section 1.1.169c
sections.original: Iam scies .
sections.translation: You shall soon know .

sections.label: Section 1.1.170
sections.original: tuos Cóllybiscus nunc in urbest vilicus ;
sections.translation: Collybiscus , your bailiff , is in the city just now . The Procurer doesn ’ t know him . Do you fully understand ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.172
sections.original: Intellego hercle , sed quo evadas nescio .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I understand that ; but what you are driving at I know not .

sections.label: Section 1.1.173
sections.original: Non scis ?
sections.translation: You don ’ t know ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.173b
sections.original: Non hercle .
sections.translation: Not I , faith .

sections.label: Section 1.1.173c
sections.original: At ego iam faxo scies .
sections.translation: But I ’ ll soon let you know . The gold shall be given him , for him to take to the Procurer ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.175
sections.original: dicatque se peregrinum esse , ex alio oppido :
sections.translation: and say that he ’ s a stranger from another city ; that he ’ s amorously inclined , and wishes to gratify his inclinations ; that he wants free range to be found him , where he may secretly indulge his appetite , so that there may be no overlooker . The Procurer , greedy for the gold , will at once take him into his house ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.180
sections.original: celabit hominem et aurum .
sections.translation: he ’ ll conceal the man and the gold .

sections.label: Section 1.1.180b
sections.original: Consilium placet .
sections.translation: The design pleases me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.181
sections.original: Rogato , servos veneritne ad eum tuos .
sections.translation: Do you then enquire of him whether your slave hasn ’ t come to him . He ’ ll think that I am being sought for ; immediately he ’ ll say no to you . Have you any doubt but that the Procurer will at once have to double the gold for you , and be considered to have stolen the man ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.185
sections.original: neque id unde efficiat habet : ubi in ius venerit ,
sections.translation: Neither has he the means of raising it . When he comes to trial , the Praetor will award [*] his whole household to you . Thus with a pitfall shall we deceive the Procurer Lycus .

sections.label: Section 1.1.188
sections.original: Placet consilium .
sections.translation: The design pleases me .

sections.label: Section 1.1.188b
sections.original: Immo etiam , ubi expolivero ,
sections.translation: Aye , when I ’ ve polished it up , you ’ ll then say so still more even ; now it ’ s but in the rough .

sections.label: Section 1.1.190
sections.original: Ego in aedem Veneris eo , nisi quid vis , Milphio .
sections.translation: I ’ m going to the Temple of Venus , unless , Milphio , you wish for anything . It ’ s the Aphrodisia [*] to-day .

sections.label: Section 1.1.191b
sections.original: Scio .
sections.translation: I know .

sections.label: Section 1.1.191c
sections.original: Oculos volo
sections.translation: I wish to amuse my eyes with the harlot finery .

sections.label: Section 1.1.193
sections.original: Hoc primum agamus quod consilium cepimus .
sections.translation: Let ’ s first proceed to this , the plan we have resolved upon . Let ’ s go in-doors , that we may instruct Collybiscus , the bailiff ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.195
sections.original: hanc perdoceamus ut ferat fallaciam .
sections.translation: how to plant this cheatery .

sections.label: Section 1.1.196
sections.original: Quamquam Cupido in corde vorsatur , tamen
sections.translation: Although Cupid has the sway in my heart , still I ’ ll listen to you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.197b
sections.original: Faciam ut facto gaudeas .
sections.translation: I ’ ll cause you to be glad it ’ s done . ( AGORASTOCLES goes into his house .) There is a speck of love upon this man ’ s breast , which cannot by any means be washed out without great harm ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.200
sections.original: itaque hic scelestus est homo leno Lycus ,
sections.translation: this Lycus , too , the Procurer , is such a wicked person , against whom the engine of mischief is now well aimed , which before long I shall discharge from my battery [*] . But see , here ’ s Adelphasium coming out , and Anterastylis . The first is the one who renders my master distracted .

sections.label: Section 1.1.205
sections.original: sed evocabo . heus , i foras , Agorastocles ,
sections.translation: But I ’ ll call him out . ( Goes to the door and calls .) Hallo ! Agorastocles , come out of doors if you would see most joyous sports !

sections.label: Section 1.1.207
sections.original: Quid istúc tumultist , Milphio ?
sections.translation: What ’ s this bustle , Milphio ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.207b
sections.original: Em amores tuos ,
sections.translation: Why , here ’ s your mistress , if you ’ d like to see her .

sections.label: Section 1.1.208b
sections.original: O multa tibi di dent bona ,
sections.translation: O may the Gods bestow many a blessing on you , for having presented to me a sight so charming as this !

sections.label: Section 1.2.210
sections.original: Negóti sibí qui volét vim paráre ,
sections.translation: The man who wants to find abundance of employment for himself — a woman and a ship , these two things , let him procure ; for no two things do produce more trouble , if , perchance , you begin to equip them ; neither are these two things ever equipped enough ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.215
sections.original: neque eis ulla ornandi satis satietas est .
sections.translation: nor is the largest amount of equipment sufficient for them . And as I mention these things , from experience at home do I now say thus ; for we two , even from daybreak up to the present hour of the day , have never ceased

sections.label: Section 1.2.220
sections.original: lavari aut fricari aut tergeri aut ornari ,
sections.translation: either washing , or scrubbing , or rubbing , or dressing , smoothing , polishing , painting , trimming , with all our might , and at the same time the two maid-servants , that have been provided for each of us , have been giving us their assistance in washing and cleaning ; and in carrying water two men have become quite weary .

sections.label: Section 1.2.225
sections.original: apage sis , negoti quantum in muliere una est .
sections.translation: Fie upon it ! how great a plague there is in one female . But if there are two , I know full well that they are able to give to any one , the mightiest nation whatsoever , more trouble than enough , in being night and day , always , at all hours , dressing , washing , rubbing , polishing .

sections.label: Section 1.2.230
sections.original: postremo modus muliebris nullust : numquam
sections.translation: In fine , there ’ s no moderation in women , nor do we understand how ever to set a limit to washing and scrubbing . But she who is washed clean , unless she is thoroughly dressed , in my notion at least , is just as though she were dirty .

sections.label: Section 1.2.233
sections.original: Mirór equidem , sóror , te istaec sic fabulari ,
sections.translation: I really wonder , sister , that you talk in this fashion ; you who are so knowing , and discreet , and clever ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.235
sections.original: nam quom sedulo munditer nos habemus ,
sections.translation: for when with all care we have ourselves in trim , hardly and with difficulty do we find poor pitiful admirers .

sections.label: Section 1.2.237
sections.original: Ita est . verum hoc unum tamen cogitato :
sections.translation: Such is the fact ; but still reflect upon this one thing ; a limit is best to be observed in all things , sister ; all things in excess give too much trouble to mortals of themselves .

sections.label: Section 1.2.240
sections.original: Sorór , cogitá , amabo , itém nos perhibéri
sections.translation: Sister , prithee , do reflect that we are accounted just in the same way as pickled salt-fish [*] is thought of — without any relish and without sweetness ; unless full oft and long it is soaked in water , it smells badly , and is salt , so that you cannot touch it .

sections.label: Section 1.2.245
sections.original: itém nos sumús
sections.translation: Just so are we . Women of this class are utterly tasteless , and devoid of grace , without dress and expense .

sections.label: Section 1.2.248
sections.original: Coqua ést haec quidem , Ágorastoclés , ut ego opínor :
sections.translation: She surely is a cook , Agorastocles , according to my notion ; she knows how to soak pickled fish .

sections.label: Section 1.2.249b
sections.original: Quid molestu'smolestus es ?
sections.translation: Why are you thus troublesome ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.250
sections.original: Soror , parce , amabo : sat est istuc alios
sections.translation: Sister , do , there ’ s a dear , forbear . It ’ s quite sufficient for others to say that to us , not to be ourselves as well proclaiming our foibles .

sections.label: Section 1.2.252
sections.original: Quiésco .
sections.translation: I ’ ll have done , then .

sections.label: Section 1.2.252b
sections.original: Ergo amó te . sed hóc nunc respónde
sections.translation: I thank you : but now answer me this ; are all things here which ought to be provided for propitiating the Gods ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.254b
sections.original: Omnia áccuravi .
sections.translation: I ’ ve taken care of everything .

sections.label: Section 1.2.255
sections.original: Diém pulchrum et célebrem et venústatis plénum ,
sections.translation: How charming and joyous a day , and full of delight , worthy of Venus , by my troth , whose Aphrodisia are celebrated to-day !

sections.label: Section 1.2.257
sections.original: Ecquid gratiae , quom huc foras te evocavi ?
sections.translation: Any thanks for me , for calling you out of doors ? Oughtn ’ t I now to be presented with a cask of old wine ? Say it shall be given . Don ’ t you answer me ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.260
sections.original: lingua huíc excidít , ut ego opínor .
sections.translation: His tongue has fallen out , I imagine . What , plague on it , have you been struck with amazement standing here ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.261b
sections.original: Síne amem , ne opturba ac tace .
sections.translation: Do let me love on ; don ’ t disturb me , and do hold your peace .

sections.label: Section 1.2.262
sections.original: Taceo .
sections.translation: I ’ ll hold my peace .

sections.label: Section 1.2.262b
sections.original: Si tacuisses , iam istuc taceo non gnatum foret .
sections.translation: If you had held your peace , why then that I ’ ll hold my peace would not have been in existence .

sections.label: Section 1.2.263
sections.original: Eamus , mea soror .
sections.translation: Let ’ s go , my sister .

sections.label: Section 1.2.263c
sections.original: Rogas ?
sections.translation: Do you ask me ? Because our master is waiting for us at the Temple of Venus .

sections.label: Section 1.2.264b
sections.original: Maneat pol . mane .
sections.translation: Let him wait , i ’ faith . Do you stay ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.265
sections.original: turba est nunc apud aram . an te ibi vis inter istás versarier
sections.translation: there ’ s a crowd just now at the altar . Do you wish yourself to be pushed about among those common prostitutes , the doxies of bakers , the cast-offs of the spelt-bread sellers ; wretched creatures , daubed over with grease [*] , followers of poor slaves , who stink for you of their stable and stall [*] , their seats and very sheds ; whom , in fact , not a single freeman has ever touched or taken home with him ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.270
sections.original: servolorum sordidulorum scorta diobolaria ?
sections.translation: the twopenny strumpets [*] of dirty trumpery slaves ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.271
sections.original: I in malam crucem . tun audes etiam servos spernere ,
sections.translation: Away with you to utter perdition ! Do you dare , then , to despise the slaves , you hussey ? As if she was a beauty , as if kings were in the habit of making her their choice . A monstrosity of a woman ! Diminutive as she is , she does spit out such mighty words — seven nights with whom I wouldn ’ t purchase at a cupful of vapour .

sections.label: Section 1.2.275
sections.original: Di immortales omnipotentes , quid est apud vos pulchrius ?
sections.translation: Immortal and omnipotent Divinities , what is there among you more beauteous ? What have you that I should deem you more immortal than I am myself . in beholding with my eyes these delights so great ? But Venus is not Venus ; for my own part , her will I worship as Venus ; that she may love me and prove propitious . Milphio !— hallo ! Milphio , where are you ?

sections.label: Section 1.2.279b
sections.original: Assum ápud te , eccum .
sections.translation: See , here I am with you .

sections.label: Section 1.2.279c
sections.original: At ego elixus sis volo .
sections.translation: But I want you boiled [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.280
sections.original: Enim vero , ere , facis delicias .
sections.translation: Why really , master , you are making merry .

sections.label: Section 1.2.280b
sections.original: De tequidem haéc didici omnia .
sections.translation: Why , it was from yourself I learnt all this .

sections.label: Section 1.2.281
sections.original: Etiamne ut ames eam quam numquam tetigeris ?
sections.translation: What , even to be in love with her whom you have never touched ? Really , that is nonsense .

sections.label: Section 1.2.281b
sections.original: Nihil id quidemst :
sections.translation: I ’ faith , the Gods as well do I love and fear from whom , nevertheless , I keep off my hands .
